title: Getting Started with React 18: What You Need to Know
content:
React 18 is Here

React 18 brings Concurrent Rendering, Automatic Batching, Transitions, and new hooks like useId and useTransition.

Key Features

  • Automatic Batching — state updates grouped automatically
  • Transitions — mark non-urgent updates with startTransition
  • Suspense on the server — stream HTML progressively

Upgrading is straightforward: replace ReactDOM.render with createRoot and you're done.

React 18 is the result of years of research into concurrent rendering.

title: The Minimalist Design Principles Every Developer Should Know
content:
Less is More

Minimalism in design is not about removing features — it's about removing everything that doesn't serve a purpose.

Core Principles

  1. White space is your friend — breathing room reduces cognitive load
  2. Limit your palette — 2-3 colours maximum
  3. Typography hierarchy — size and weight guide the eye
  4. One action per screen

For every element ask: Would the user miss this if it was gone? If not, remove it.

title: Backpacking Southeast Asia on a Budget: My 3-Month Journey
content:
The Trip That Changed Everything

Three months, six countries, less than $3,000. Southeast Asia is the most budget-friendly travel destination in the world — if you know how.

Countries Visited

  • 🇹🇭 Thailand — Bangkok, Chiang Mai, Koh Tao
  • 🇻🇳 Vietnam — Hanoi to HCMC by overnight train
  • 🇰🇭 Cambodia — Angkor Wat at sunrise
  • 🇱🇦 Laos — Luang Prabang
  • 🇲🇾 Malaysia — KL and Penang
  • 🇸🇬 Singapore — 3 days
The best moments were never the ones I planned.

title: Understanding Large Language Models: A Practical Introduction
content:
What Are LLMs?

Large Language Models are neural networks trained on massive text corpora. They learn statistical patterns to predict the next token — and that simple objective leads to surprisingly powerful emergent capabilities.

Key Concepts

  • Tokens — subword units the model processes
  • Context window — how much text the model can see
  • Temperature — controls randomness in generation
  • Fine-tuning vs prompting — two ways to adapt a model to a task

title: From Zero to First Customer: What Nobody Tells You About Startups
content:
The Unglamorous Truth

Everyone talks about funding rounds. Nobody talks about six months of cold emails that get no reply.

What Actually Matters

  1. Talk to users before writing any code
  2. Revenue over users — focus on paying customers
  3. Do things that don't scale — manual onboarding, personal emails
  4. Hire slow, fire fast
Build something 100 people love, not something 1 million people kind of like.

title: Python Type Hints: Writing Cleaner, Safer Code
content:
Why Type Hints Matter

Python is dynamically typed but that doesn't mean you give up type safety. Type hints let you annotate variables and functions — tools like mypy catch bugs before runtime.

def greet(name: str) -> str:
    return f'Hello, {name}'

Useful Patterns

  • Optional[T] — value might be None
  • TypedDict — type-annotated dicts
  • Protocol — structural subtyping (duck typing + safety)

title: My Figma Workflow for Designing at Speed
content:
Design Fast Without Cutting Corners

After 5 years designing products I've settled on a Figma workflow that takes me from blank canvas to handoff specs in hours.

Core Setup

  • Component library first — atoms before molecules
  • Auto-layout everything
  • Variables for colours and spacing

The biggest productivity unlock: learn keyboard shortcuts for everything. Mouse clicks are expensive.

title: Notes on MongoDB Aggregation Pipelines
content:
Beyond find()

For analytical queries the aggregation pipeline is where MongoDB's real power lies.

Key Stages

  • $match — filter documents
  • $group — aggregate
  • $project — reshape
  • $lookup — join collections
db.orders.aggregate([
  { $match: { status: 'completed' } },
  { $group: { _id: '$user_id', total: { $sum: '$amount' } } },
  { $sort: { total: -1 } }, { $limit: 10 }
])

title: Mastering TypeScript Generics: A Deep Dive
content:
Why Generics?

Generics let you write functions and classes that work with any type while still giving you full type safety.

function first<T>(arr: T[]): T | undefined {
  return arr[0]
}

Real-world Patterns

  • Generic API responses — ApiResponse<User>
  • Generic repository pattern — Repository<T>
  • Constrained generics — T extends Serializable

Once you understand generics, you'll use them everywhere.

title: Docker for Developers: From Zero to Production
content:
Why Docker?

Docker solves the classic 'it works on my machine' problem by packaging your app and all its dependencies into a portable container.

Core Concepts

  • Image — a read-only template for creating containers
  • Container — a running instance of an image
  • Dockerfile — instructions for building an image
  • docker-compose — run multi-container apps
FROM python:3.11-slim
WORKDIR /app
COPY requirements.txt .
RUN pip install -r requirements.txt
COPY . .
CMD ["python", "run.py"]

title: Git Workflows Every Team Should Know
content:
Why Git Workflow Matters

A bad Git workflow causes merge conflicts, broken main branches, and deployment nightmares. A good one makes the team invisible to each other.

Three Popular Workflows

  1. GitHub Flow — simple, branch from main, PR, merge. Best for CI/CD.
  2. Gitflow — develop/release/hotfix branches. Good for versioned software.
  3. Trunk-based development — everyone commits to main with feature flags.

For most web teams, GitHub Flow wins. Keep it simple.

title: Building REST APIs with Flask and MongoDB
content:
Flask + MongoDB = Fast APIs

Flask's simplicity combined with MongoDB's flexibility makes for a powerful API stack.

Project Structure

backend/
  app/
    routes/
    models/
    utils/
  run.py

A Simple Route

@app.route('/api/users', methods=['GET'])
def get_users():
    users = db.users.find({})
    return jsonify(list(users))

Use Flask blueprints to keep routes organised as the API grows.

title: WebSockets Explained: Real-time Apps from Scratch
content:
HTTP is Half the Picture

HTTP request-response works great for most apps — but for real-time features like chat, live dashboards, or multiplayer games, you need WebSockets.

How It Works

WebSockets start with an HTTP upgrade handshake, then maintain a persistent bidirectional connection.

With Flask-SocketIO

@socketio.on('message')
def handle_message(data):
    emit('reply', {'text': data['text']}, broadcast=True)

Socket.IO adds auto-reconnect, rooms, and namespaces on top of raw WebSockets.

title: CSS Grid vs Flexbox: When to Use Each
content:
Two Tools, Different Jobs

Both Flexbox and Grid are layout systems, but they're designed for different dimensions of layout.

Use Flexbox for

  • One-dimensional layouts (row OR column)
  • Navigation bars, button groups, card headers
  • Aligning items within a container

Use Grid for

  • Two-dimensional layouts (rows AND columns)
  • Page-level layout, card grids, dashboards
  • Complex overlapping elements

In practice: Grid for the outer layout, Flexbox for the components inside it.

title: Next.js vs Remix: Choosing the Right React Framework
content:
Both Are Great — For Different Reasons

Next.js and Remix are both React meta-frameworks. The choice depends on your architecture preferences.

Next.js Strengths

  • Mature ecosystem, massive community
  • App Router + React Server Components
  • Vercel deployment is effortless

Remix Strengths

  • Web standards first (uses native Request/Response)
  • Excellent nested routing and data loading
  • No waterfalls — loaders run in parallel

For most new projects in 2024, Next.js App Router wins on ecosystem. But Remix's mental model is cleaner.

title: Securing Your Web App: OWASP Top 10 Explained
content:
The OWASP Top 10

The Open Web Application Security Project maintains a list of the most critical security risks. Every developer should know them.

The Big Three

  1. Injection — SQL, NoSQL, command injection. Always sanitise input.
  2. Broken Authentication — use JWTs correctly, short expiry, rotate secrets
  3. Sensitive Data Exposure — HTTPS everywhere, never log passwords, hash with bcrypt

Quick Wins

  • Content Security Policy headers
  • Rate limiting on auth endpoints
  • CORS configured to your domain only

title: Dark Mode Design: Best Practices and Common Mistakes
content:
Dark Mode is Harder Than It Looks

Dark mode is more than inverting colours. Done wrong, it causes eye strain and accessibility issues.

Best Practices

  • Don't use pure black — use dark greys like #121212
  • Reduce contrast slightly — white on black is harsher than white on dark grey
  • Elevate surfaces with lightness — higher components should be lighter
  • Test with images — photos look different in dark contexts

Implementation

CSS custom properties with prefers-color-scheme is the cleanest approach.

title: Color Theory for Developers: Building Better UIs
content:
Color Is a Language

Understanding color theory makes you a significantly better UI designer, even if you're primarily a developer.

The Basics

  • Hue — the colour itself (red, blue, green)
  • Saturation — intensity (vivid vs. muted)
  • Lightness — how bright the colour is

Practical Rules

  1. Pick one primary, one secondary, one neutral palette
  2. Use saturation to show importance, not just colour
  3. Ensure 4.5:1 contrast ratio for accessibility (WCAG AA)

title: Typography Fundamentals Every Web Developer Should Know
content:
Typography Makes or Breaks a UI

Bad typography makes even beautiful designs feel cheap. Good typography is invisible — you just feel the content.

The Four Fundamentals

  1. Type scale — use a modular scale (1.25x or 1.333x)
  2. Line height — 1.5-1.7 for body, 1.1-1.25 for headings
  3. Line length — 60-75 characters for optimal readability
  4. Font pairing — one serif + one sans usually works well

For the web, Inter + a serif like Playfair Display is a reliable pairing.

title: A Week in Kyoto: Japan on a Budget
content:
Kyoto in Autumn

If you visit Japan once in your life, make it Kyoto in November. The autumn foliage transforms every temple into a painting.

Where to Go

  • Arashiyama — bamboo grove and Tenryu-ji at golden hour
  • Fushimi Inari — go at 6am to beat the crowds
  • Philosopher's Path — canal walk lined with maple trees
  • Nishiki Market — the best street food in Japan

Budget Tips

Get a 7-day JR Pass before you leave home. Stay in a guesthouse — the communal breakfasts are worth it.

title: Road Trip Through Portugal: 10 Days, 1200 km
content:
Lisbon to the Algarve and Back

Portugal is one of Europe's most underrated road trip destinations — great roads, stunning scenery, and a fraction of the cost of France or Italy.

The Route

  1. Lisbon (2 nights) — Belém tower, LX Factory, pasteis de nata
  2. Évora (1 night) — Roman temple, bone chapel
  3. Lagos (2 nights) — sea caves, Ponta da Piedade
  4. Sintra (1 night) — fairy-tale palaces on the way back

Rented a car for €35/day with full insurance. Best decision of the trip.

title: The Ultimate Bali Guide for First-Time Visitors
content:
Everything You Need to Know Before You Go

Bali gets a bad reputation for being overrun with tourists. That's true in the south. But most of the island is still magical.

Skip / Visit

  • ❌ Skip Kuta — Seminyak is slightly better, Canggu is much better
  • ✅ Visit Ubud — rice terraces, cooking classes, the best coffee
  • ✅ Visit Sidemen — untouched valley, incredible trekking
  • ✅ Visit Nusa Penida — dramatic cliffs, Kelingking Beach

Practical Notes

Rent a scooter — it's the only way to move freely. Budget: $40-60/day for a comfortable stay.

title: Exploring Patagonia: The End of the World
content:
The End of the World

Patagonia is unlike anywhere else on earth. The scale is incomprehensible until you're standing in it.

The W Trek (Torres del Paine)

Five days, four camps, one of the world's great hikes. Book huts 6 months in advance or you'll be sleeping in a tent in 60mph winds.

Practical Notes

  • Fly into Punta Arenas, bus to Puerto Natales
  • Weather changes every 30 minutes — pack layers
  • The park entrance fee is $35 — worth every penny
There are no words. You have to go.

title: Digital Nomad Life in Lisbon: A 6-Month Review
content:
Why Lisbon?

I chose Lisbon for my first long-term nomad base because of the weather, the cost of living, the timezone (GMT in winter), and the coffee.

Six Months Later

  • Cost — €1,800/month for a good flat in Mouraria, all bills included
  • Coworking — Second Home is beautiful but expensive. Heden is better value
  • Community — Nomad List Lisbon Slack is active and welcoming
  • Downsides — rental market is brutal, the hills are real

title: How to Write a Cold Email That Actually Gets Replies
content:
The Email Nobody Opens

Most cold emails fail not because of bad offers, but because of bad execution. Here's the framework that got me a 34% reply rate.

The Five-Line Formula

  1. Line 1: Specific compliment (show you did research)
  2. Line 2: Why you're reaching out (one sentence)
  3. Line 3: Your relevant credibility (not your full CV)
  4. Line 4: Your ask (make it tiny)
  5. Line 5: Easy reply (yes/no question)

Subject line: keep it under 6 words. No clickbait. Be specific.

title: Product-Market Fit: How to Know When You Have It
content:
You'll Know When You Have It

Product-market fit is described as a feeling as much as a metric — users pulling the product out of your hands instead of you pushing it on them.

Leading Indicators

  • Retention curves flatten instead of trending to zero
  • NPS above 40 with genuine promoters
  • Organic referrals without you asking
  • Support requests that are about adding features, not fixing broken ones

The Sean Ellis Test

Ask users: 'How would you feel if you could no longer use this product?' If 40%+ say 'very disappointed', you have it.

title: Building a Personal Brand as a Developer
content:
Your Brand is What People Say When You're Not in the Room

As a developer, your personal brand is built in public — through your code, your writing, your interactions online.

Three Pillars

  1. Consistency — post about one or two topics, not everything
  2. Generosity — give away 90% of what you know for free
  3. Specificity — 'React developer who writes about performance' beats 'developer'

Where to Build

Pick one platform and go deep. Twitter/X for real-time, LinkedIn for professional reach, a personal blog for long-form authority.

title: Remote Work Culture: What We Got Right After 3 Years
content:
Three Years of Remote-First

We've been fully remote since before it was forced on everyone. Here's what we actually got right — and what took years to figure out.

What Worked

  • Async by default — write decisions down, don't schedule a meeting
  • Camera on for 1-on-1s — presence matters for relationships
  • Quarterly in-person — remote works because of the occasional in-person

What Didn't Work

Trying to replicate the office on Slack. The real-time noise killed deep work for everyone.

title: Python for Data Analysis: Getting Started with Pandas
content:
Why Pandas?

Pandas is the Swiss Army knife of data analysis in Python. If you're working with tabular data, you'll use it every day.

The Essentials

import pandas as pd
df = pd.read_csv('data.csv')
df.head()           # first 5 rows
df.describe()       # summary statistics
df['col'].value_counts()  # frequency count

Filtering and Grouping

high_value = df[df['amount'] > 1000]
by_region = df.groupby('region')['sales'].sum()

Start with these three operations and you can answer 80% of business questions.

title: SQL Window Functions: The Feature That Changed How I Query
content:
Window Functions Changed Everything

Before I learned window functions, I was writing subqueries and temporary tables for things that should take one line.

The Syntax

SELECT name, salary,
  AVG(salary) OVER (PARTITION BY dept) AS dept_avg,
  RANK() OVER (ORDER BY salary DESC) AS salary_rank
FROM employees;

Most Useful Functions

  • ROW_NUMBER() — unique row numbers within a partition
  • LAG() / LEAD() — access previous/next row values
  • SUM() OVER () — running totals

title: Building a Simple Recommendation System with Python
content:
Recommendation Systems Don't Have to Be Complex

Netflix and Spotify use deep learning. You probably don't need that. Here's a simple collaborative filtering approach that works surprisingly well.

User-Based Collaborative Filtering

from sklearn.metrics.pairwise import cosine_similarity
import pandas as pd

ratings = pd.DataFrame(user_ratings)
similarity = cosine_similarity(ratings.fillna(0))

Find users similar to your target, aggregate their ratings weighted by similarity. Simple, explainable, effective.

title: How I Learned to Code in 6 Months (Without a CS Degree)
content:
No Degree, No Problem

I went from zero coding experience to a junior developer role in 6 months. Here's the exact path I took.

The Curriculum (Self-Imposed)

  1. Months 1-2: Python fundamentals + basic algorithms
  2. Month 3: HTML, CSS, basic JavaScript
  3. Month 4: React fundamentals, build 3 small projects
  4. Month 5: One full-stack project (deployed publicly)
  5. Month 6: Job applications, open source contributions, networking

The key is shipping projects. Certificates don't get you hired. Projects do.

title: The Art of Code Review: Giving Feedback That Actually Helps
content:
Code Review is a Skill

Most developers give code review feedback like they're marking a test — looking for things to correct. The best reviewers teach through their feedback.

The Golden Rules

  1. Review the code, not the person — 'this function does X' not 'you did X'
  2. Ask questions, don't always prescribe — 'What do you think about...'
  3. Praise good code — don't only comment on problems
  4. Limit blocking nits — not every style choice needs a comment

A great code review feels like a conversation, not a critique.

title: CSS Custom Properties: The Modern Way to Theme Your App
content:
CSS Variables in 2024

CSS custom properties have been supported in all major browsers for years. If you're still hardcoding colour values, it's time to stop.

:root {
  --primary: #7c3aed;
  --surface: #12122a;
  --text: #e8e8f8;
}

.btn-primary {
  background: var(--primary);
}

Dark Mode with One Toggle

[data-theme='dark'] {
  --surface: #0d0d1a;
  --text: #e8e8f8;
}

Variables cascade — change one value at the root and everything updates instantly.

title: Freelancing as a Developer: My First Year in Numbers
content:
The Numbers (Full Transparency)

I left my full-time job 14 months ago. Here's exactly how the finances played out — the good, the bad, and the stressful months in between.

Revenue by Quarter

  • Q1 — €4,200 (2 clients, mostly panic)
  • Q2 — €8,100 (referral kicked in)
  • Q3 — €12,400 (first retainer client)
  • Q4 — €15,800 (month 12 was my best ever)

What I'd Tell Myself on Day 1

Charge more. Say no to bad-fit clients sooner. Build your audience while you still have a salary to fall back on.
